commits.message:
mit-story-5rm.3: give the descent floor-dark one home — a --field-floor token

[LAW:one-source-of-truth] the floor-dark #100e16 was the one dark-field value
that had escaped the file's own ':root' tokenisation principle ("every hex
lives once, here"): it was inlined across every scene gradient AND hardcoded
in the threshold's JS veil, and the new surfacing veil copied that JS literal —
a third home for one fact. Promote it to a canonical --field-floor custom
property at :root; point the scene gradients at var(--field-floor); and route
BOTH seam veils through color-mix(in srgb, var(--field-floor) N%, transparent),
the codebase's existing color-mix idiom, so the JS painters name only an alpha
and never re-state the colour. One declaration, derived everywhere.

Verified live in Chrome: --field-floor resolves to #100e16; both veils render
color(srgb 0.0627 0.0549 0.0863 / a) === rgba(16,14,22,a), pixel-identical to
the prior literals; scene gradients resolve var(--field-floor) to rgb(16,14,22);
zero console errors.

mit-story-5rm.3: the surfacing — the epilogue rises out of the descent's dark back into paper, mirroring the hero

The descent↔epilogue seam was a hard cut; the entry threshold dramatized
its mirror seam but the exit had no counterpart. Add a 'surfacing' part —
the inverse of 'threshold' — that paints the descent field's floor-dark
(#100e16) as a veil over the epilogue, thinning from full at the rise to
nothing at pin, so the window gives way back to paper and the closing words
resolve into legibility as the page surfaces. [LAW:decomposition] it is its
own seam part obeying the painter contract (measure/progress/paint) WITHOUT
being a node, composed into the single tick() loop by ONE uniform call off
the shared scrollY read — never special-cased into the generic node loop.
[LAW:single-enforcer] it owns ONLY the section background veil; the back-to-
top link's opacity stays the outro's (its exit), so the link surfaces purely
because the veil thins behind it. [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow] pure
position-driven colour, no transform, no autonomous animation, no
REDUCE_MOTION branch — reduced-motion-safe by construction.

The back-to-top is now the loop made visible: a vertical reflection of the
hero's scroll hint — line-rising→arrow↑→word against the hero's
word→arrow↓→line-dropping — so the first and last frames read as a
deliberate pair, and #top literally re-enters the inception.

Verified live in Chrome: veil alpha 1.0→0.5→0.0 across the rise (smoothstep),
continuous with the dark descent above, pristine paper at pin; zero console
errors.

mit-story-5rm.2: the descent threshold — paper gives way to the window as the first frame rises (#46)

The hero is light paper; the first framed scene is a dark window that, untouched,
slides up over it as a hard wipe. This dramatizes the crossing as the inception
conceit's first beat: a `threshold` module — the entry mirror of the `outro`'s
exit bookend — that, as the first frame rises to pin, thickens a veil of the
descent field's own floor-dark (#100e16) over the cover, so the paper visibly
gives way to the window instead of being razor-cut by it. The scroll hint
extinguishes the instant the descent takes hold (by p≈0.45), its one word obeyed.

[LAW:decomposition] the seam is its own part: a measure/progress/paint module that
mirrors the painter contract WITHOUT being a descent node, composed into the single
scroll owner by ONE uniform call alongside the outro — never special-cased into the
generic node loop. [LAW:single-enforcer]

[LAW:no-ambient-temporal-coupling] the crossing is scroll-progress data flowing
through the existing single paint loop in tick(), off the one scrollY read — no
parallel timer, no one-off listener. [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow] both writes are
pure scroll-position state (colour + opacity, no transform, no autonomous animation),
so the module has no REDUCE_MOTION branch: it IS the designed static threshold
reduced-motion asks for, the same path running in both modes.

Verified live (Chrome DevTools): veil thickens smoothstep 0->1 across the last
viewport of the cover's exit, hint gone by p~0.45, the seam dissolves to seamless
window-dark at completion; zero console errors.
